paragraphs.text: We are pleased to submit our comments to this public consultation. ASML recognises the importance of ensuring a fair and transparent digital environment and of protecting consumers from manipulative or exploitative practices. However, many of the concerns addressed in the possible Digital Fairness Act are already covered by existing EU and national legislation. Rather than adding a new layer of overlapping obligations, in our view, the priority should be to promote consistent and effective implementation of the current framework. Additional regulation risks creating complexity and uncertainty for businesses, while offering limited added value for consumers. A coherent and streamlined legal environment is key to also supporting European competitiveness. New legislation should only be introduced after ensuring that existing rules are effectively applied to these issues.

It is essential first to make full use of existing legislation. Article 25 of the Digital Services Act (DSA) directly prohibits deceptive or manipulative online interfaces by online platforms. The practices listed in Article 25(3) DSA already overlap with those identified in the Commission's Fitness Check. Even non-binding guidance would significantly enhance legal certainty and support consistent compliance across the digital ecosystem.

evidence_quotes.quote: Ordinary persuasion and dialogue that are part of traditional marketing and sales promotion should not be interpreted as dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: Ordinary persuasion and dialogue that are part of traditional marketing and sales promotion should not be interpreted as dark patterns. In all forms of human interaction, regular persuasion is a natural and fundamental element of dialogue. The concept of dark patterns should not be broadened to include ordinary commercial practices.

The criteria for defining a dark pattern must be grounded in an objective and factual assessment. A criterion that is by its nature subjective and speculative is unsuitable as a normative standard. For this reason, the legislative text should include a clarification ensuring that standard lawful commercial persuasion is not considered a dark pattern.

Legislation based on the ePrivacy Directive, GDPR (e.g. Articles 5, 9, and 12--14), DSA (Articles 27 and 28), AI Act, and consumer protection law, already provides consumers with extensive safeguards in this area.

evidence_quotes.quote: ordinary personalisation of services and communications does not, in practice, involve the exploitation of vulnerabilities.
Ordinary personalisation has always been, and remains, an essential part of good service and customer relationship management.
Minors are also generally not the target of personalisation.

paragraphs.text: It should be noted that ordinary personalisation of services and communications does not, in practice, involve the exploitation of vulnerabilities. Ordinary personalisation has always been, and remains, an essential part of good service and customer relationship management. Minors are also generally not the target of personalisation.

evidence_quotes.quote: profiling is recognised as a normal and legitimate part of personal data processing.
personalisation of a service and communications is increasingly an inherent element in the performance of a contract.

paragraphs.text: It should also be emphasised that under the GDPR, profiling is recognised as a normal and legitimate part of personal data processing. Moreover, personalisation of a service and communications is increasingly an inherent element in the performance of a contract.

Regulatory measures designed for very large online platforms should not automatically be extended to other ordinary companies. Asymmetric regulation for major platforms remains a justified and proportionate structure, reflecting their unique market position and impact.
New rules should be evidence-based and supported through an assessment of the effectiveness of the current legislation mentioned.

evidence_quotes.quote: regulatory measures on age verification aimed at protecting minors are proportionate

paragraphs.text: It is essential that any regulatory measures on age verification aimed at protecting minors are proportionate and do not unduly restrict access to services for other users.

evidence_quotes.quote: age verification laws would be too costly and operationally impossible.

paragraphs.text: In the US state of Mississippi, the social media platform BlueSky announced that it could no longer provide its services there, as complying with the new strict age verification laws would be too costly and operationally impossible. This example illustrates the potential unintended consequences of overly burdensome regulatory requirements.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
